coredata.dc:title: Two-photon fluorescence-guided precise photothermal therapy located in a single cancer cell utilizing bifunctional N-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Dan
Huang, Kai
She, Jiahong
Cai, Yuying
Liu, Boyuan
Wei, Zhongchao
Chen, Yibo
Huang, Jinqing
Fan, Haihua
coredata.dc:description: The utilization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for photothermal therapy has emerged as a hot research topic. However, there has been limited research on killing one single cancer cell which is critical in reducing unnecessary damage to the surrounding healthy tissues. In this work, we developed a two-photon fluorescence-guided precise photothermal therapy in a single human malignant melanoma (A375) cancer cell utilizing bifunctional N-doped CQDs. Resulting from the two-photon fluorescence of the CQDs, one single cancer cell can be located and simultaneously destroyed by the photothermal effect of the same CQDs. Specifically, the balanced two-photon absorption cross-section (7000 GM) and photoluminescence quantum yield (8.4%) of the CQDs enable the fluorescence-guided photothermal treatment to be achieved in only 5 s under the irradiation of 800 nm laser of 27.5 mW, much faster than the control experiment without the guidance of fluorescence. The heat generated by the aggregated CQDs is in sufficient amounts while being confined in a small area, as evidenced by the numerical simulations and photothermal experiments, to limit the range of thermal treatment in the cells. This work provides a new approach for realizing photothermal therapy with minimal damage and establishes a new application scenario of CQDs for precise tumor ablation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Two-photon fluorescence
Carbon quantum dots
Photothermal therapy
Precise tumor ablation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Biocompatibility and bio-imaging in A375 cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectrum, (b) XRD patterns, (c) HRTEM images of the CQDs. (d) Absorption spectrum, (e) single- and two-photon excited fluorescence spectra of CQDs, showing as the black line and the red line, respectively. (f) Power dependence of two-photon excited fluorescence integrated intensity of CQDs under an 800 nm femtosecond laser. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) HRTEM image of an A375 cell slice incubated with CQDs. (b) Enlarged HRTEM image of CQDs naturally aggregated in A375 cell. (c) Viability of A375 cells incubated with different concentrations of CQDs measured by MTT assay. (d) Images obtained by an inverted microscope of A375 cells incubated with different concentrations of CQDs.

Images of the A375 cells culturing (a-c) without CQDs and (d-f) with CQDs. (a, d) Bright field image. Two-photon fluorescence image in (b, e) green channel and (c, f) blue channel. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) TEM image of the CQDs distributed in the cytoplasm. (b) CQDs model built based on the TEM image. (c) The electric-field enhancement (E/E0) map of the CQDs at 800 nm laser excitation, with a selected subregion in the upper right corner of the figure for ease of observation. (d-f) Contour maps of the temperature distribution of CQDs under the irradiation of 800 nm laser of (d) 12.5 mW, (e) 20.0 mW, and (f) 27.5 mW.

(a, b) Fluorescence-guided photothermal ablation of a single A375 cell under irradiations (femtosecond laser, 800 nm, 5 s) with different powers. (c) Photothermal therapy of a single A375 cell under femtosecond laser (800 nm, 30 s) without fluorescence-guiding. After the photothermal treatment, the cells were dyed with Trypan Blue. (d) Comparison of the elapsed time in two treatments. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of CQDs as a fluorescent-photothermal bifunctional PTT agent for a FGPTT process under laser irradiation.

head.ce:title: Two-photon fluorescence-guided precise photothermal therapy located in a single cancer cell utilizing bifunctional N-doped carbon quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Clear Water Bay, Hong Kong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The utilization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for photothermal therapy has emerged as a hot research topic. However, there has been limited research on killing one single cancer cell which is critical in reducing unnecessary damage to the surrounding healthy tissues. In this work, we developed a two-photon fluorescence-guided precise photothermal therapy in a single human malignant melanoma (A375) cancer cell utilizing bifunctional N-doped CQDs. Resulting from the two-photon fluorescence of the CQDs, one single cancer cell can be located and simultaneously destroyed by the photothermal effect of the same CQDs. Specifically, the balanced two-photon absorption cross-section (7000 GM) and photoluminescence quantum yield (8.4%) of the CQDs enable the fluorescence-guided photothermal treatment to be achieved in only 5 s under the irradiation of 800 nm laser of 27.5 mW, much faster than the control experiment without the guidance of fluorescence. The heat generated by the aggregated CQDs is in sufficient amounts while being confined in a small area, as evidenced by the numerical simulations and photothermal experiments, to limit the range of thermal treatment in the cells. This work provides a new approach for realizing photothermal therapy with minimal damage and establishes a new application scenario of CQDs for precise tumor ablation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the fatal diseases to human health, cancer treatment has gained more and more attention. Photothermal therapy (PTT) has emerged as a prominent area of research due to its ability to selectively target tumor tissues with greater precision than conventional therapies [1–4]. This targeted approach minimizes damage to healthy tissues and enhances treatment efficacy, making it a highly promising avenue for cancer treatment [5–7]. Typically, near-infrared (NIR) light is used as a light source instead of ultraviolet light because of its good penetration ability and its harmlessness to biological tissues [8,9]. In addition, to minimize the damage to the surrounding tissues, optical materials with nonlinear absorption in the NIR region are primarily chosen as the photothermal conversion medium since the nonlinear absorption of photons always occurs only near the focal point of the light source. However, the most reported materials with multiphoton-absorption performance and applicable for bio-related applications, such as silver- and gold-based nanomaterials, [10–13] and organic fluorescence molecules [14,15], unfortunately, suffer from high cost, poor biocompatibility, and intrinsic instability.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials that are typically smaller than 10 nm in size. With characteristics of low biological toxicity, excellent water dispersity, stable fluorescence, adjustable excitation/emission wavelength, and abundant surface modifications [16–20], CQDs have shown great potential in the fields of cell imaging, drug delivery, optoelectronic devices [21–27], and so on. In 2007, the multiphoton-excited luminescence of CQDs was first reported and successfully applied to the bioimaging of MCF-7 cells, which opened the door for CQDs to be used in bio-imaging and optical therapy [28]. Since then, a lot of research in this domain has emerged. For instance, Yuan et al. synthesized CQDs with green fluorescence and applied them to image mouse liver at a depth of 440 µm under a femtosecond laser [29]. Zheng et al. synthesized carbon dots (named CyCD) from a hydrophobic cyanine dye and achieved tumor destruction under irradiation from an 808 nm laser at 2 W/cm2 in 10 min [30]. Zhao et al. synthesized CQDs with yellow fluorescence and then used them to induce the apoptosis of HeLa cells by continuous laser irradiation (635 nm, 2 W/cm2) for 10 min [31]. However, prolonged irradiation from high-energy lasers will inevitably damage the surrounding healthy cells in the photothermal treatment of tumors [32,33], which severely limits the practical application of CQDs.

Herein, we developed a novel two-photon fluorescence-guided photothermal therapy (FGPTT) approach utilizing N-doped CQDs as a fluorescent-photothermal bifunctional agent and performed a precise ablation on a single human malignant melanoma (A375) cell as a proof of concept (Scheme 1 ). The bifunctional CQDs emit two-photon fluorescence under the excitation of an 800 nm femtosecond laser, which enables the precise localization of vesicles in a single cancer cell. Meanwhile, the CQDs convert the laser energy into heat, leading to the efficient killing of individual cancer cells. The two-photon fluorescence and photothermal performance of the CQDs are appropriately balanced, thanks to their favorable two-photon absorption cross-section (7000 GM) and proper photoluminescence quantum yield (8.4%). Notably, only 5 s of irradiation at a low power of 27.5 mW is sufficient to achieve immediate ablation of the single cancer cell, which is significantly faster than the control experiment without the guidance of fluorescence. Compared to conventional photothermal therapy, our novel FGPTT approach exhibits a substantial improvement in treatment efficiency. Furthermore, we thoroughly investigated the detailed optical, fluorescent-imaging, and photothermal performance in our FGPTT system using both experimental and theoretical methods, which will provide implications for the future advancement of precise and efficient cancer treatments.

CQDs were synthesized according to our previous report [34]. First, 2.0 g of citric acid and 2.0 g of urea were dissolved and mixed in 10 mL of distilled water. The mixed solution was then heated in a microwave oven with 650 W power for 5 min. Next, the obtained dark brown product was dispersed in 10 mL of distilled water and centrifuged at 10000 rpm to collect the supernatant. To further purify the CQDs, the supernatant was dialyzed three times in distilled water using semipermeable membranes (MWCO = 1.0–10.0 KD).

The cytotoxicity of CQDs to A375 was determined by Thiazolyl Blue Tetrazolium Bromide (MTT) assay. The absorbance value of each well was measured by the enzyme-labeled instrument (Bio-Rad iMark, America).

The COMSOL Multiphysics software developed by COMSOL Inc. (https://www.comsol.com) was employed for the numerical simulation. The carbon quantum dots were constructed as a sphere model with a diameter of 7.86 ∼ 13.86 nm, and a 2.5 μm × 2.5 μm × 0.3 μm water-filled cuboid was used to simulate the cell environment. The real part (n) and the imaginary part (k) of the refractive index were set to be 1.39 and 0.34748, respectively. The value of the constant pressure heat capacity was fitted using a graphite constant pressure heat capacity curve [35,36]. The initial temperature was set to 37 °C to simulate the normal human body temperature. Under laser irradiations with different power, the steady-state temperature distribution map and instantaneous temperature change map of carbon quantum dots clusters in human cells were obtained by simulating the process of carbon quantum dots heating up and dissipating heat to the surrounding environment.

The A375 cells were first seeded into the culture dishes and incubated for 24 h in a humidified incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2). Then, the culture medium containing CQDs of 0.742 μg/mL was added to the experimental group while the same amount of culture medium without CQDs was added to the control group. After culturing for 24 h, the A375 cells were washed with the PBS solution, and a drop of fresh culture medium was added to keep the cells alive in the imaging experiment. Under the excitation of an 800 nm femtosecond laser, the two-photon fluorescence images of A375 cells were obtained by using a Laser scanning microscopy (Nikon A1, Japan).

The photothermal therapy experiments were conducted using an inverted microscope (Axio Observer A1, Zeiss) and a laser scanning microscope (Nikon A1, Japan), with a femtosecond (fs) laser light generated from a Ti: sapphire oscillator (Mira 900S, Coherent) with a repetition rate of 76 MHz was applied as a light source. The culture medium containing CQDs of 7.42 μg/mL was used to incubate A375 cells for 24 h in a humidified incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2). The two-photon images of A375 cells incubated with CQDs were first obtained by scanning with a laser at low power to locate the CQDs. After that, a laser with power above the cell damage threshold was focused on the position of the located CQDs in the cell. At last, the Trypan Blue (TB) solution was added to the culture dish to check whether the A375 cells were alive or not since the dead cells would become blue while the alive ones were not. Please find details of the chemicals information, physicochemical characterizations of CQDs, and cell culture in the Supplementary Information file.

The physicochemical characterizations demonstrate successful synthesis of the mono-dispersed CQDs. The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) in Fig. 1
a shows the functional groups on surface of the CQDs. Abundant hydrophilic groups such as OH (3420 cm−1), CO (1693 cm−1), and NH (3182 cm−1 and 1183 cm−1) can be designated, resulting in good dispersity of the CQDs in aqueous solution. Moreover, the spectrum also shows the characteristic peaks of CH (3051, and 2768 cm-1), CC (1575 cm-1), and CN (1354 cm-1), consistent with the typical functional groups of CQDs in previous reports [37,38]. A wide diffraction peak appears at 2θ = 29° in X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 1b), indicating highly disordered carbon atoms and an amorphous carbon phase [39]. The upward shift compared to the typical (002) peak around 26.5° is attributed to the highly defective carbon structure [40]. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1c) reveals that the as-prepared CQDs have a mono-dispersed morphology, and the size distribution map (Fig. S1) indicates that the size of CQDs is concentrated in 6.0 ∼ 9.0 nm. Besides, the lattice fringes of the CQDs with a spacing of 0.21 nm can be attributed to graphene carbon, consistent with the XRD results. As shown in Table. S1, the elements C and O are the main components of the CQDs. The appearance of the Cu element is due to the impact of the copper grid, and the other elements with a small proportion may be introduced when the sample is made.

Both favorable single- and two-photon optical performance of the CQDs were demonstrated. As shown in Fig. 1d, the CQDs have a strong absorption in 340 ∼ 460 nm. To study the single- and two-photon fluorescence of CQDs, the 400 nm near-ultraviolet light and 800 nm femtosecond laser were applied as the excitation source, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1e, under the irradiation of 400 nm near-ultraviolet light, the CQDs show a broad emission band peaking at 510 nm. The photoluminescence quantum efficiency of the CQDs under NUV light is calculated to be 8.4% (Table S2). Under the irradiation of an 800 nm femtosecond pulse laser, a similar broad emission is also observed. The emission peak redshifts to 538 nm because of the photon self-absorption caused by deeper penetration of NIR photons [41]. To further explore the two-photon fluorescence of CQDs, we recorded the fluorescence intensity under 800 nm femtosecond laser with different powers and plotted them in logarithm (Fig. 1f). According to the formula I ∼ PK [42] in which I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, P is the excitation power of the laser, and K is the exponent of the power function, the value of K is fitted to be 2.18. It indicates that the fluorescence of the CQDs under the excitation of an 800 nm laser is induced by a two-photon absorption process. The two-photon absorption cross-section is calculated to be 7000 GM (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and bio-imaging in A375 cells
ce:section.ce:para:
After A375 cells were cultured with CQDs, cell slices were made to further observe the intracellular distribution of CQDs. The HRTEM image (Fig. 2
a) shows a visible cell (red line). The areas (region b) of concentrated distribution of carbon quantum dots can be seen. A magnified view of region b was taken (Fig. 2b) and it shows heavily aggregated CQDs in the cytoplasm and vesicles. Since the CQDs enter the cell mainly through endocytosis [43], the dots possibly enter the lysosome after incubation for 24 h [44,45]. The elemental distribution of the quantum dots observed inside the cell in Fig. 2b is shown in Fig. S2 and Table S3. It can be seen that the distribution is similar to the bare CQDs, which is shown in Table S1. The higher O element content comes from the intracellular oxide. The standard 3-(4-pyrroli-dine-5-dimethylthiazolyl-2-yl)-2-pyrrolidine-5diphenyltetrabrozole (MTT) assay was used to quantify the cell viability after 24 h of incubation. Fig. 2c (left) demonstrates that there is a maximum increase of 6.17% for cell viability at a low concentration of CQD (0.371 µg/mL). As carbon is an essential element for cell formation, a small amount of CQDs may promote the growth of cells. Fig. 2c (right) shows the results of culturing cells with higher concentrations of CQDs from 3.71 to 18.55 µg/mL. The cell viability remains 92.15% under 3.71 µg/mL of CQDs. Observed by an inverted microscope, the cells mostly show spindle shapes and are densely distributed (Fig. 2d), which demonstrates high cell viability. As the concentration of CQD increases, more and more spherical cells appear, implying apoptosis of the cells. This means that the cell viability gradually decreases as the concentration increases, but there are still a large number of cells in the field of view, proving that the cells still have good viability even when the concentration of CQDs reaches 18.55 μg/mL. Based on the results, we comprehensively chose 3.71 μg/mL as the most appropriate culture concentration because of the relatively high cell survival rate of 92.5%.

Two-photon fluorescence bioimaging performance of the CQDs is investigated under the culture concentration of 3.71 μg/mL. For the two-photon fluorescence peak of the CQDs located at 538 nm, green and blue channels were selected to record the fluorescence intensity of the control cells and the experimental cells. Fig. 3 shows that the fluorescence intensity of the A375 cell taking up CQDs is much stronger than that of the control group. It can be seen From Fig. 3e and 3f that small amounts of CQDs also enter the nucleus. The nucleophilicity of the CQDs might be due to the small particle size, and their stable and hydrophilic properties in cells. The fluorescence intensity values of the control and experimental cells in green and blue channels are shown in Table S4. Due to the fluorescence of CQDs, the average brightness of the whole cell is increased by 2.7 times compared to the control group. More importantly, the area of the strongest fluorescence marks the aggregation location of the CQDs, which is highly conducive to the precise photothermal process under the guidance of fluorescence.

The photothermal performance of the CQDs was evaluated by theoretical simulation and experiments. For theoretical simulation, a model of CQDs aggregated in a cell is first made according to the TEM image of the cell slice (Fig. 4
a and Fig. S3). As shown in Fig. 4b, the area of the region where the CQDs are aggregated is about 300 nm × 300 nm, and the size and spatial distribution of the CQDs are set according to the TEM image. Fig. 4c shows the distribution of near-field intensity, with the polarization direction of the incident laser marked. The field enhancement of CQDs is concentrated in the area where they are in close contact with each other. This suggests that the primary cause of heat generation in CQDs is the carbon core, while the reason for their fluorescence is mainly due to the functional groups decorating the carbon core. Moreover, as mentioned earlier, CQDs tend to aggregate in cells, which provides favorable conditions for their heat generation. In the simulation of the actual temperature field, 800 nm femtosecond lasers with powers of 12.5 mW (Fig. 4d), 20.0 mW (Fig. 4e), and 27.5 mW (Fig. 4f) were selected, respectively. As the power increases, the temperature of the area where CQDs are aggregated rises significantly. At 12.5 mW, the highest temperature reaches 78 °C, and it reaches as high as 125 °C at 27.5 mW. Such high temperatures can easily kill a single cell. It should be noted that the irradiation time we set is very short, so the effect of water phase transition was neglected when calculating the temperature field. Meanwhile, as the laser power increases, the temperature of the central area rises significantly, but there is little change in the temperature of the edge region, ensuring that the use of CQDs for photothermal therapy will not cause damage to surrounding cells. On the other hand, we also simulated the case of low-density carbon quantum clusters (Fig. S4). At 12.5 mW, the highest temperature reaches 56.8 °C, and it reaches as high as 72.7 °C at 27.5 mW. Compared to the case of low-density carbon quantum clusters, high-density carbon quantum clusters can generate more heat but still in a small region.

To further verify the photothermal conversion capability of the CQDs, the temperature variation of the CQDs solution (1 mg/mL) was also measured under irradiation of an 800 nm femtosecond laser (0.4 W) with pure water as a control. As shown in Fig. S5 a, the temperature of the CQDs solution increases rapidly from 26 to 47 °C under the femtosecond laser irradiation at the first minute and remains stable after reaching 54 °C, fully meeting the requirements of tumor photothermal therapy. In comparison, the highest temperature of the pure water only reaches 37 °C, which is 17 °C lower than that of the CQDs solution, demonstrating the excellent photothermal performance of the CQDs. The measured temperatures are not as high as those obtained from the theoretical simulations because the theoretical model uses the heat capacity of graphite as the initial parameter, which only comprises the inner core of the practical CQDs. In three repeated heating experiments (Fig. S5b), the performance of CQDs exhibited similarity. Therefore, it can be inferred that the stability of CQDs is commendable.

In this work, the two-photon imaging was combined with the photothermal therapy performance of the CQDs to create a technique which is called fluorescence-guided photothermal therapy (FG-PTT). It was conducted on a single A375 cell. The bright-field and the two-photon fluorescence image at the same moment taken in Fig. 5 a show the activity of the cells and the labeling effect of the CQDs. Based on the fluorescence images obtained by two-photon scanning microscopy, the areas of CQDs aggregation were clearly marked and then the located areas were irradiated for 5 s with different power levels (Fig. 5b). It can be seen that the cell morphology changes from spindle-shape to spherical after a while. Afterward, the results of trypan blue staining (Fig. 5c) showed that the located cell died immediately after the irradiation with a 27.5 mW laser. With laser irradiation at lower powers of 20.0 mW and 12.5 mW, the cells died 5 and 10 min after laser treatment, respectively. Photothermal therapy (PTT) without the guide of fluorescence on a single A375 cell was performed to make a comparison with FGPTT. The bright-field images of A375 cells obtained by inverted microscopy are shown in Fig. 5c. Based on it, one viable A375 cell was selected and irradiated at each power using a femtosecond laser for 30 s. According to the results after the staining of Trypan blue, the cells died 60 and 20 min after being treated with 12.5 and 20.0 mW lasers respectively, while died immediately at 27.5 mW (Fig S6-S8). Meanwhile, the control cells were still alive at 27.5 mW (Fig. S8). Fig. 5d shows the results of the treatment comparison between the two methods, with PTT having a longer treatment time (30 s) and a longer onset (maximum time required is 60 min) of action, and FG-PTT having a shorter treatment time and a faster onset (maximum time required is 10 min) of action. More importantly, the efficiency enhancement is more obvious at lower powers, which means less damage to the surrounding health tissues and a safer treatment. Herein, the use of CQDs for FGPTT enables more efficient and precise killing of individual cells, showing great potential for photothermal ablation applications.

In summary, monodispersed N-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with a narrow size distribution (6.0 ∼ 9.0 nm) were synthesized through a one-step process and employed as a bifunctional light-to-light/light-to-heat medium in a precise FGPTT approach on a single A375 cell. The CQDs emit fluorescence at 538 nm upon 800 nm excitation with a two-photon absorption cross-section of 7000 GM, resulting in a 2.7-fold increase in fluorescence intensity in the testing cells compared to the control cells. Furthermore, the photothermal conversion ability of CQDs was verified by both theoretical simulations and experimental results. Benefitting from the favorable two-photon fluorescence and photothermal performance of the CQDs, a precise photothermal ablation of a single A375 cell was accomplished within 5 s of laser irradiation and onset for a maximum of 10 min, while the control experiment required 30 s of irradiation and onset for 60 min. This work develops a new application scenario for CQDs in precise photothermal therapy and fully illustrates the advantages of FGPTT in terms of high efficiency and effective avoidance of unnecessary damage.

Dan Li: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Kai Huang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Jiahong She: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Yuying Cai: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Boyuan Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Zhongchao Wei: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yibo Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jinqing Huang: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Haihua Fan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (Grant No. 2021A1515010352), and Guangzhou University - Hong Kong University of Science and Technology Joint Research Collaboration Fund Project (Grant No. YH202209).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.02.114.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
